title: FI Port Channel Health
exclusions: Cisco Intersight–managed (or Intersight-primary) Fabric Interconnect domains, where Northbound LAN port-channel health is not represented through the classic UCSM XML/HTTPS statistics poll this add-on uses; Fibre Channel SAN port-channels in `fabric/san/…/fabricFcPc` (different MO tree from the Ethernet LAN LAGs covered here); upstream Nexus vPC, routing, and STP control-plane events that the FI never surfaces as a `fabricEthLanPc` `operState` failure; and the internal IOM/server-facing LAGs outside the `fabric/lan/A|B/pc-*` Northbound `dn` pattern used in the base SPL (those paths need their own, chassis-focused runbook).
dataSources: `index=cisco_ucs` (or your org-standard index) and sourcetype `cisco:ucs:stats` from `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` (Splunkbase 2731, v4.3.1). The add-on's **Cisco UCS statistics** modular input runs on a Heavy Forwarder (or an equivalent data-collection node) and polls the UCSM XML/HTTPS API for managed statistics object classes — including the Ethernet LAN port-channel aggregate `fabricEthLanPc`, the per-member `fabricEthLanPcEp` endpoints, and (for bps and error companion panels) the physical-port `etherPIo` rows with `etherTxStats`, `etherRxStats`, and `etherErrStats`. Parallel UCSM syslog (routed to the same team-standard sourcetype pattern as the fault stream in UC-19.1.1) is recommended for text link-up/link-down events, even though the primary signal in this use case comes from the stats feed.
app: `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` ([Splunkbase 2731](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731), current release 4.3.1, Splunk-supported on 9.1–10.2 and Splunk Cloud). The on-disk and canonical add-on id uses an underscore (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`); some older download bundles may still show the hyphenated `Splunk_TA_cisco-ucs` in a filename, but that is a packaging artefact, not a different product.
spl:
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:stats" earliest=-30m
| search (classId="fabricEthLanPc" OR classId="fabricEthLanPcEp")
| where match(dn, "^fabric/lan/(A|B)/pc-")
| eval oper=lower(trim(coalesce(operState, oper_state, op_state, "")))
| eval parent_pc=if(classId="fabricEthLanPc", dn, replace(dn, "/ep-[^/]+$", ""))
| rex field=parent_pc "^fabric/lan/(?<fi_fabric>[AB])/pc-(?<pc_id>[^/]+)$"
| eval is_ep=if(classId="fabricEthLanPcEp", 1, 0)
| eval is_pc=if(classId="fabricEthLanPc", 1, 0)
| eval member_up=if(is_ep=1 AND (oper="up" OR oper="link-up" OR oper="online"), 1, 0)
| sort 0 - _time
| stats
    latest(eval(if(is_pc=1, oper, null()))) as channel_oper
    max(is_pc) as has_pc
    sum(is_ep) as configured_members
    sum(member_up) as active_members
  by parent_pc, fi_fabric, pc_id
| eval active_pct=if(configured_members>0, round(100*active_members/configured_members, 0), 100)
| eval channel_degraded=if(!isnull(channel_oper) AND channel_oper!="up" AND channel_oper!="link-up" AND channel_oper!="online", 1, 0)
| eval member_degraded=if(configured_members>0 AND active_pct<100, 1, 0)
| eval health=case(channel_degraded=1, "Degraded (channel oper)", member_degraded=1, "Degraded (member)", 1=1, "Healthy")
| eval needs_attention=if(health!="Healthy", 1, 0)
| table parent_pc, fi_fabric, pc_id, channel_oper, configured_members, active_members, active_pct, health, needs_attention
| sort 0 - needs_attention, fi_fabric, pc_id
description: Detects Cisco UCS Fabric Interconnect Ethernet LAN Northbound port-channels (managed-object class `fabricEthLanPc`, with members in `fabricEthLanPcEp`) whose aggregate `operState` is not `up`, or where fewer than all LACP member endpoints are up in the same `cisco:ucs:stats` poll window. Uses a `dn`-based filter (`^fabric/lan/(A|B)/pc-`) to scope to Northbound uplink LAGs and ignore the unrelated server-facing and Fibre Channel SAN LAG paths that are out of scope for this use case.
value: A degraded or down FI uplink LAG is a class-of-service incident for the whole UCS domain on that fabric side — not a single-VM noisy-neighbour problem. Storage multipath, vMotion, and bare-metal tenants on service profiles can all see coherent loss when Northbound path diversity drops or the entire LAG goes down. Catching the condition in the same UCSM-backed data the platform and data-centre networking teams already trust gives operations a single place to begin triage, before dozens of application on-calls open the same P1 with vague "network is down" clues.
implementation: Enable the Cisco UCS statistics modular input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` so `cisco:ucs:stats` is being indexed, confirm with `| stats count by classId` that `fabricEthLanPc` and `fabricEthLanPcEp` are present, then schedule the saved `spl` (optionally appending `| search needs_attention=1` for a pure alert) on a 5- or 10-minute cadence. Pair with Nexus and UCSM syslog streams for the same time window in war-room dashboards.
visualization: (1) Status grid: one tile per UCSM cluster × `fi_fabric` × `pc_id`, coloured by `health`. (2) Bar chart of `active_pct` per `parent_pc` over the last 30 minutes. (3) Single value of port-channels with `needs_attention=1` (and a second tile for `channel_degraded=1` only). (4) Table of degraded rows with `channel_oper`, `configured_members`, `active_members`, and `active_pct` for operator drilldown. (5) Timechart of aggregate uplink throughput from a companion search on `etherTxStats`/`etherRxStats` for the member `etherPIo` ports in the same A/B fabric scope — not from the `fabricEthLanPc` row alone, which does not carry per-port counters.

references.title: Cisco UCS Manager Network Management Guide, Release 4.2 (port-channels, LAN fabric)
Integrate data with Splunk: Cisco UCS (Splunk documentation)
Cisco UCS Manager XML API Reference, Release 4.2 (managed objects, fabricEthLanPc class)

knownFalsePositives: Short **LACP renegotiation and rebalancing** after an authorised member add or removal — `active_pct` can dip for one or two polls while forwarding is intentionally de-preferenced on the Nexus side for hash stability, even though UCSM and Splunk both show a degraded mathematical count. **Spanning-tree convergence and BPDU-guard recovery** after an upstream Catalyst or Nexus supervisor reload can discard a VLAN for tens of seconds while the LACP bundle on the FI still reports a logically up aggregate — pair this UC with switch-layer evidence before you RMA the wrong line card. **vPC ISSU or a vPC peer-link maintenance** on the upstream pair can re-home a single member to the standby path while the bundle toward your FI still carries traffic; this is not a Splunk false positive, it is a reminder that Nexus health is not encoded in UCSM alone. **Administratively shutting a facing member** on the Nexus side during a cable pull or A/B test will trigger this alert, which is expected in the ticket but noisy in the inbox if your change-calendar lookup is missing Nexus node names or the change-record number. **Single-member LAGs in test or DR pods** are valid by policy (one NIC in service), so a `<100%` test is nonsensical there — either filter those `dn` values with a small allow-list, or treat the policy as a governance issue, not a monitoring bug. **Mistaking a Fibre Channel `fabric/san/…/pc-*` for an Ethernet uplink** because someone deleted the LAN regex from the search is a configuration error that the `match(dn, ...)` clause in the base SPL is specifically there to prevent. **FI line-card or supervisor restarts during a Cisco firmware bundle** can pause stats polls for longer than a Nexus-side maintenance window — a brief "no rows in Splunk" gap is not automatically a dark data centre if Nexus `ifOperStatus` and the UCSM GUI both stay green; require a second signal before paging on stats silence alone. **Clock skew** between UCSM, the forwarder, and the indexer can reorder clear and raise events in raw tables; for Sev1, compare `_time` to the UCSM local clock in the GUI footer.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731, v4.3.1 or newer) on a Heavy Forwarder (or any tier where modular inputs are allowed to run), with outbound HTTPS 443 to the UCS Manager cluster virtual IP. The add-on (or a TA-only app bundle) is also needed on Search Heads for knowledge objects, saved searches, and field discovery.
• A read-only UCSM service user whose role can read the LAN fabric and port-channel managed-object classes — at minimum `fabricEthLanPc` (the Ethernet LAN port-channel aggregate) and `fabricEthLanPcEp` (each member endpoint on that aggregate). Do not bind collection to a named human administrator. Rotate the credential on the schedule your security team already uses for other API integrations.
• Index choice: the examples use `cisco_ucs` because that matches the Splunk and Cisco field guides. If you route UCS events to a different index, keep the sourcetype the TA issues for statistics — normally `cisco:ucs:stats` — so this library, your `props.conf`, and your on-call runbook all agree on a single name.
• Licensing and volume: `cisco:ucs:stats` is poll-driven. Volume scales with the enabled MO class list, the poll interval, and the number of objects in the domain. Budget by sampling one hour of License Usage and the `_internal` add-on log before you set sub-minute schedules everywhere.
• Network path: the collection host must trust the UCSM TLS server certificate. Import the full chain, not just the leaf. Corporate TLS forward-proxies are acceptable if you install the re-signing CA in the same trust store the `splunkd` process uses. Captive portals and HTTP-only redirect chains that strip API POST bodies are not supportable — they will look like 401, 403, or odd timeouts in `splunkd.log`.
• Architecture caveat: a healthy FI-local `operState` does not prove that an upstream Nexus vPC domain is perfect. A vPC peer-link flap or a spanning-tree reconvergence that isolates a switch pair is often invisible in FI-only data; pair this UC with network-layer visibility when the incident bridges teams.
• Northbound vs server-side channels: Northbound Ethernet LAN `pc-*` objects live at `fabric/lan/A` or `fabric/lan/B` and represent the data-centre LAG to Nexus-class peers in typical designs. Server-port style IOM and chassis paths use different DNs. The base SPL anchors on `^fabric/lan/(A|B)/pc-` and explicitly excludes those internal namespaces from the "uplink is dying" on-call this use case is meant to trigger.
• Why `cisco:ucs:stats` (and not a fictional `cisco:ucs:fi_stats`): the add-on does not list a `cisco:ucs:fi_stats` sourcetype in the canonical set. The statistics feed for MO classes like `fabricEthLanPc` is carried under `cisco:ucs:stats`. If a dashboard author pastes a search that references a sourcetype your deployment never ingests, operators conclude "no data" and give up — even when the real feed is a few menu clicks away. Always confirm with the discovery probes: `index=cisco_ucs | stats count by sourcetype` and, once you know stats exist, `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype=cisco:ucs:stats earliest=-1h | stats count by classId` to see the exact `classId` values your TA build emits before you finalise the saved search. Field spelling (`operState` vs `oper_state`) is normalised in the SPL with `coalesce()`; extend that list with whatever names you observe in a five-minute `head` sample, not a guess from a blog post.
• Parallel syslog (triage): the primary health signal here is `cisco:ucs:stats`, but you should also forward UCSM system and port syslog to an operations-appropriate index. A `link-down` text line in syslog landing within a minute of a stats gap is a powerful cross-check when a poll is late. Route syslog to the same logical sourcetype patterns you use for the fault stream in UC-19.1.1 so the fabric team is not learning a second bespoke name every week.

**MO and stats vocabulary** (keep this consistent across teams): `fabricEthLanPc` is the LAG aggregate; `fabricEthLanPcEp` is one row per LACP member; `etherPIo` is a physical Ethernet port; `etherTxStats`, `etherRxStats`, and `etherErrStats` are throughput/error counters on those `etherPIo` rows. The line-rate bps view in this UC comes from a companion search starting at the member `etherPIo` DNs, not from treating `fabricEthLanPc` as a 32-bit counter by itself. Keep FC SAN LAG objects (`fabricFcPc` under `fabric/san/…`) separate — they are the storage team's story, not a drop-in for this LAN uplink use case.

**Step 1 — Enable statistics collection and verify the fields before building the alert**
(1) Install `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` on the collection Heavy Forwarder and on the search tier for lookups, exactly as the vendor guide describes for your Splunk version.
(2) In Splunk Web → **Settings → Data inputs** under the Cisco UCS family (the wording varies slightly with minor releases), enable a **Cisco UCS statistics** modular input pointed at `https://<ucsm-fqdn-or-vip>`, and bind the destination index to `cisco_ucs` unless your data-governance standard already chose another approved name.
(3) In the same configuration, ensure the poll includes the port-channel aggregate and member classes — not just generic `ether*Stats` on unbundled ports. Without `fabricEthLanPc` + `fabricEthLanPcEp` you cannot count LACP members in Splunk and you are back to guessing.
(4) Restart the forwarder (or reload only) per the add-on release-note guidance, using your existing deployment tool so you never have one stray host with a half-enabled profile.
(5) After two poll cycles, run the discovery probes from the Prerequisites section, then a raw preview: `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype=cisco:ucs:stats earliest=-15m (classId="fabricEthLanPc" OR classId="fabricEthLanPcEp") | head 20`. Inspect the **Fields** sidebar and `_raw` for the authoritative spelling of the operational-state field.
(6) If you rename the sourcetype in `local/props.conf` to something that is not `cisco:ucs:stats`, every search in the public library will miss your site until you add a `union` or change your standard back. Do not do that in production without an architecture note your auditors can read.
(7) If you are running UCSM in active/standby or active/active with paired FIs, keep separate rows for `fabric A` and `fabric B` in your mental model — many incidents are single-sided with N+1 capacity still in play.

**Step 2 — Build the health search, wire it to a dashboard, and attach an alert action**
(1) Use the `spl` field of this use case as the base of a saved search.
(2) The search scopes by Distinguished Name, not by a non-existent `object_type="port_channel"` label that might appear in synthetic examples. Real deployments expose `classId` for managed-object classes and the TA maps those into the event; always align filters with a `classId` list pulled from the `stats` probe, not from an older slide deck.
(3) Operational state is lower-cased and read through a `coalesce(operState, oper_state, op_state, "")` pattern. If your `head` output shows a fourth spelling, add it. Treat `link-up` as healthy where your UCSM and NX-OS design expects it; if your runbook is stricter, narrow the set in a governed change, not in an unreviewed one-line edit.
(4) Parent keys: for each `fabricEthLanPcEp` row, `replace(dn, "/ep-[^/]+$", "")` recovers the same `fabric/…/pc-N` string that the aggregate row uses — that is the key operators search for in the UCSM tree.
(5) Member percentage: `active_members / configured_members * 100` is rounded for readability. The alert condition fires on `<100%` to catch a suspended or err-disabled LACP child even when the aggregate still reports a partially-up state. Validate that behaviour in a test change window, because your vendor and STP settings may make a one-member LAG a by-design two-NIC vPC.
(6) Alert schedule: a 5- or 10-minute cadence is typical; the `earliest` window should cover at least one full stats poll, not a five-second ad-hoc range that only ever sees a partial class subset.
(7) Notification body: pass `parent_pc`, the A/B fabric, `pc_id`, `channel_oper`, and `active_pct`, plus a short sentence telling the on-call to open the UCSM path from Step 3.
(8) Suppression should key on the composite of cluster + `parent_pc` + a short hash of the degraded reason so an LACP retrain that flips between member and channel does not file three tickets.
(9) Do not rely on a dashboard tile colour as your only out-of-hours signal — if your organisation requires SMS or voice, route through the same on-call platform you already trust for other UCS alerts. (Avoid the generic "wire every alert to email only" hand-wave; follow your own secure notification standard.)

**Step 3 — Validate in UCSM on the right screen, with a concrete failure test**
(1) Copy a Degraded row's `parent_pc` into your clipboard — for example, `fabric/lan/B/pc-12`.
(2) In UCSM, navigate **Equipment → Fabric Interconnects →** pick Fabric A or Fabric B to match the second path segment, then the correct Fixed Module or Expansion Module (depends on 6324/6332/6454/6536 and line-card layout), and open the Port-Channels list for that module scope. Some releases also expose a top-level list — use whichever your standards doc names, as long as the `pc-*` you click is the same numeric ID as in the `dn`.
(3) You should see the LACP bundle's operational state, the list of members, and on each member row the physical `ether` port that backs the corresponding `fabricEthLanPcEp` object. Match those members to the counts in Splunk; if a member is `admin-down` on the NX-OS side, UCSM and Splunk can still look partial while the change ticket is the real system of record.
(4) For a lab test, during an approved window, shut or unchannel a non-essential member on the Nexus side only, watch the STP and LACP messages on the switch, then confirm Splunk's `active_pct` drops below 100 on the next one or two polls. Roll back the change before you leave the console.
(5) Fibre Channel `fabric/san/…/pc-*` does not belong to the LAN SPL — if your alert misfires for SAN names, recheck that the only DNs reaching your alert pass the `^fabric/lan/(A|B)/pc-` test.
(6) Intersight-led or cloud-managed FIs (see the `exclusions` field) can stop emitting classic UCSM stats once migration happens; a quiet Splunk index then is a governance signal, not proof that the physical links are perfect.

**Step 4 — Operationalise: hand-offs, capacity, and evidence retention**
(1) Build the dashboard panels described in the `visualization` field, including a timechart of aggregate Northbound bps from the member `ether*Stats` classes for capacity conversations. Keep that on a different row from the red `health` tile so the audience is not told a link is down when it is only full.
(2) Hand-off rule: UCSM and Nexus teams both own parts of a vPC incident. The first page from this UC is still UCSM-backed, but the bridge call must include a Nexus bridge if Splunk's `channel_oper` is up while applications are down — that pattern almost always means an upstream problem.
(3) Change calendar: a lookup keyed on Nexus node names is more accurate than a plain date range for "we are doing switch ISSU". Empty lookup rows must never silently suppress a brand-new, unannounced loss of LAG — default to page-first until the lookup process is itself audited.
(4) Evidence: for Sev1, export the saved-search output table with `_time` and the UCSM screenshot from Step 3 into your incident tool in the first hour, not a week later when Splunk's Search Job may have expired.
(5) Runbook URLs: the Cisco UCSM Network Management guide referenced in `references` is the long-form help for port-channel and LAN-fabric behaviour; the XML API book is a field reference. Keep both in the same incident template so a network engineer and an automation author do not talk past each other.
(6) KPI hygiene: a Healthy LAG with rising bps and zero errors is a capacity-programme input, not a false negative for this UC. Split those dashboard rows so a capacity manager does not unmute a healthy LAG that is only busier.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
• **No `cisco:ucs:stats` rows at all** — statistics input disabled, wrong index in your role, 401/403 to UCSM, or TLS path broken. Re-run the global sourcetype counts; fix trust store and credential rotation before you tune SPL.
• **Stats exist but no `fabricEthLanPc*`** — the statistics profile is too narrow. Add the port-channel aggregate and member-endpoint classes in the add-on configuration, wait two polls, re-run the `classId` table.
• **Member counts are always zero** — the time window is shorter than one poll, or a replication lag exists between cluster peers. Widen the diagnostic `earliest` to `-4h` once, fix the real issue, then return to a tight page window.
• **`active_pct<100` during a controlled Nexus-side admin-down of one leg** — often a true positive in the narrow sense, but a governed change. Reconcile with the change record before a page, not only after.
• **Spanning-tree reconvergence after a Nexus reload or uplink bounce** — 30–120 seconds of member churn and discarding is common. A two-poll confirmation rule or a short alert delay prevents noise when business SLA still holds.
• **vPC ISSU on a Nexus pair** — may flap only the facing vPC member port toward one FI, while the port-channel toward the other vPC peer still looks up. The FI is not a substitute for the Nexus "who is my peer" story.
• **LACP renegotiation after a member add or remove on a live bundle** — expect a short window where the hash and distributing state are not final; the port-channel is not necessarily failed.
• **FI software activation or line-card restart during an FI firmware bundle** — stats silence for seconds to a few minutes can be normal. Do not duplicate Sev1 on only a stats gap; confirm with syslog or a second NOC-visible signal.
• **Single-member LAGs in a pilot or DR pod** — `configured_members=1` with `active_members=1` gives `active_pct=100`, which is correct. If your policy is "always two members," that is a governance check in UCSM, not a percentage test in this UC.
• **Server-port (IOM) internal bundles appearing in the alert** — someone removed the LAN regex from the SPL. Restore the `^fabric/lan/(A|B)/pc-` anchor and re-test.
• **Read-only user can see Equipment in the UCSM GUI but not via API** — RBAC differs between UI and API. Fix the role on the read-only user the modular input actually uses, not the personal admin you tested with in a browser.
• **Clock skew between UCSM, the forwarder, and the indexer** — can reorder clear and raise events in raw tables. For Sev1, compare `_time` to UCSM's own local clock in the GUI footer.
• **Cisco Intersight-managed domains** — if a domain has migrated to Intersight-primary management, classic UCSM polling for those FIs simply stops. The events do not disappear; the data-source has moved. Treat a sudden drop in `cisco_ucs` index volume for that cluster as a migration signal, not as proof the LAGs are healthy.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the link bundles on the two fabric switches that connect your server farm to the data-centre network. When a link goes missing in a bundle or a whole bundle fails, we raise it right away, because a bad uplink can cut the network for every server using that path at once.
hardware: Cisco UCS B200 M5/M6/M7, UCS C220 M5/M6/M7, UCS C240 M5/M6/M7, UCS C480 M5, UCS X210c M6/M7, UCS X410c M6, UCS 6324 FI, UCS 6332 FI, UCS 6454 FI, UCS 6536 FI
